title: External token callbacks can reenter before balances and market totals are updated
claim: State-changing entrypoints call external token code via `doTransferIn`/`doTransferOut` before checkpointing user principals, indexes, and market aggregates, and the contract has no reentrancy guard. A callback-capable or malicious supported token, or a recipient contract receiving tokens on `withdraw`/`borrow`, can reenter while the protocol still reflects stale balances.
impact: An attacker can repeat withdrawals or borrows against unchanged internal accounting, or reenter other state-changing functions while collateral and debt checks still use pre-transfer state. This can drain pool cash, bypass liquidity checks, or create bad debt across supported markets.
paths: `withdraw(asset, amount)` transfers out at line 1735 before reducing `supplyBalances[msg.sender][asset]`, so a recipient hook can reenter `withdraw` again against the old balance.
`borrow(asset, amount)` transfers out at line 2647 before increasing `borrowBalances[msg.sender][asset]` and `market.totalBorrows`, so a callback can borrow repeatedly before debt is recorded.

`supply`, `repayBorrow`, and `liquidateBorrow` all call `doTransferIn` before storage updates, allowing a sender-hook-based reentrant call to execute while the original operation still uses stale user and market state.

title: Self-liquidation aliases borrower and liquidator collateral balances, minting collateral to the caller
claim: `liquidateBorrow` never rejects `targetAccount == msg.sender`. In that case `supplyBalance_TargetCollateralAsset` and `supplyBalance_LiquidatorCollateralAsset` point to the same storage slot, but the function computes separate borrower and liquidator post-liquidation balances and then writes both. The second write overwrites the first.
impact: A liquidatable borrower can repay part of their own debt and finish with more collateral balance than they started with, effectively minting withdrawable protocol credit while also reducing debt. This can directly make the collateral market insolvent.
paths: A borrower becomes liquidatable, then calls `liquidateBorrow(msg.sender, assetBorrow, assetCollateral, closeAmount)`.
Because both collateral balance references alias the same storage slot, the borrower-side write stores `current - seize`, then the liquidator-side write overwrites it with `current + seize`.
The caller's debt falls by `closeAmount`, but their collateral balance increases instead of staying unchanged, letting them withdraw excess assets later.

title: Incoming-token accounting trusts the requested amount instead of the amount actually received
claim: `doTransferIn` only checks whether `transferFrom` returned success; it never compares the contract's pre/post token balance. Supply, repay, and liquidation logic therefore book the requested `amount` as cash received and as user/accounting updates even when a fee-on-transfer, deflationary, rebasing, or otherwise non-standard token causes the protocol to receive less.
impact: A supplier can receive excess internal credit and later withdraw or borrow against value the protocol never received. Likewise, a borrower or liquidator can repay less than the system records, reducing debt or seizing collateral at a discount and leaving bad debt behind.
paths: Supplying a transfer-tax token credits `amount` to the supplier and `market.totalSupply` while protocol cash increases by less than `amount`.
Repaying with a transfer-tax token reduces the borrower's debt by the full requested amount even though the market receives less cash.
Liquidating with such a token repays less than recorded but still seizes collateral based on the overstated repayment amount.

title: Suspending a borrow market makes solvent positions immediately liquidatable
claim: When `_suspendMarket` sets `markets[asset].isSupported = false`, `liquidateBorrow` deliberately skips the `discountedRepayToEvenAmount` shortfall-based cap and instead allows liquidation up to the lesser of the user's full borrow and discounted collateral value. There is no requirement that the account actually be undercollateralized once the borrow market is suspended.
impact: An admin market suspension can immediately expose healthy borrowers in that asset to third-party liquidation and collateral seizure. A risk-management or operational action therefore converts directly into user fund loss.
paths: A user holds a healthy borrow in `assetBorrow` backed by adequate collateral.
Any third party can then call `liquidateBorrow` against that user and seize discounted collateral even though the account had no shortfall before suspension.
